LAW AND MOTION, DEFENDANT BALAJI SRINIVASAN'S DEMURRERS TO THE FIRST AND FOURTH CAUSES OF ACTION IN PLAINTIFFS SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT. DEFENDANT BALAJI SRINIVASAN'S DEMURRER TO THE FIRST AND FOURTH CAUSES OF ACTION FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE AND BREACH OF THE IMPLIED COVENANT OF GOOD FAITH AND FAIR DEALING IN PLAINTIFFS LEERINK REVELATION HEALTHCARE FUND I, L.P AND LEERINK REVELATION HEALTHCARE FUND II, L.P.'S SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. PLAINTIFFS HAVE INDICATED THAT THEY ARE NO LONGER PURSUING A CAUSE OF ACTION FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE AND IT IS DISMISSED. PLAINTIFF'S ALLEGATIONS THAT MR. SRINIVASAN BREACHED THE IMPLIED COVENANT BY NOT COMPLYING PROVISIONS OF THE ROFR AGREEMENTS IS COVERED BY AND IS DUPLICATIVE OF PLAINTIFFS' BREACH OF CONTRACT CAUSE OF ACTION. BECAUSE PLAINTIFFS HAVE NOT SHOWN HOW AMENDING THE PLEADING WOULD CURE ITS DEFICIENCIES, THE DEMURRER IS SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS) ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS) JUDGE: ERIC FLEMING, CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO CSR#8600, MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM, REPORTED. =(302/EF)

Not argued, Counsel presents proposed order. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT BALAJI SRINIVASAN's Motion To Compel Responses To Special Interrogatory No. 4 From Plaintiff Revelation Healthcare Fund I, L.P. And Plaintiff Revelation Healthcare Fund Ii, L.P. is Motion granted. Sanctions not requested and not awarded. Moving party's Special Interrogatory No. 4 covers a single subject matter and therefore does not violate the "rule of 35." Clement v. Alegre (2009) 177 Cal. App. 4th 1277. This case is not at all similar to the situation in Catanese v. Superior Court (1996) 46 Cal. App. 4th 1159. Opposing parties' objections on the grounds of overbroad, compound, and vagueness/ambiguous are without merit. Opposing party appears to have "deliberately misconstrued the question." Deyo v. Kilbourne (1978) 84 Cal. App. 3d 771, 783. Opposing party is ordered to provide complete, verified responses to Special Interrogatory No. 4 within 15 days after notice of entry of this order. Sanctions not requested and not awarded.

Order is signed in open court.

DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-07-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attornesy, Christopher Stretch and Zandir Morton of the law offices of Pillsbury Winton Shaw Pittman LLP (415-983-1054) appear as counsels for plaintiff/cross-defendant/moving party

Attorney, Benjamin B. Au of the law offices of Durie Tangri (213-992-4499) appears as counsel for defendant/cross-complainant/opposing party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

CROSS DEFENDANTs LEERINK REVELATION HEALTHCARE FUND I, L.P, LEERINK REVELATION HEALTHCARE FUND II, L.P DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT, as to the sole cause of action for fraud in the inducement in the cross-complaint filed by defendant and cross-claimant Balaji Srinivasan is OVERRULED. California substantive law, not Delaware substantive law, governs the issue of the sufficiency of the reliance allegations in Mr. Srinivasan's fraud claim. Per Ron Greenspan Volkswagen, Inc. v. Ford Motor Land Development Corp. (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 985, Mr. Srinivasan has adequately alleged that he relied on the alleged fraud of the Leerink entities. California law as set forth in Ron Greenspan conflicts with Delaware law on the legal effect of anti-reliance clauses. California has a materially greater interest than Delaware in the issue of whether the anti-reliance contractual clause bars Mr. Srinivasan's fraud claim. That issue does not involve the internal affairs of Delaware corporate citizens. California's extremely strong interest in preventing the enforcement of a contract exempting a California resident corporation from responsibility for fraudulently inducing a contract in California with a California resident that was substantially performed in California is far greater than "Delaware's general, though profound, interest in vindicating freedom of contract." (EBP Lifestyle Brands Holdings, Inc. v. Boulbain (Del. Ch., Aug. 4, 2017) 2017 WL 3328363 at *8). Written order is signed and filed.

Defendant Balaji K. Srinivasans Notice Of Hearing On General Demurrers And Demurrers To The First And Fourth Causes Of Action In Plaintiffs Second Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Oct-23-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT BALAJI SRINIVASAN's Demurrers To The First And Fourth Causes Of Action In Plaintiffs Second Amended Complaint.

Defendant Balaji Srinivasan's demurrer to the first and fourth causes of action for specific performance and breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in plaintiffs Leerink Revelation Healthcare Fund I, L.P and Leerink Revelation Healthcare Fund II, L.P.'s second amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiffs have indicated that they are no longer pursuing a cause of action for specific performance and it is dismissed. Under Delaware law the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which inheres in every contract and proscribes a party's arbitrary or unreasonable conduct that prevents the other party from receiving the benefits of the bargain, "comes into play. . . only where a contract is silent as to the issue in dispute." Narrowstep, Inc. v. Onstream Media Corp. (Del. Ch., Dec. 22, 2010, No. CIV.A. 5114-VCP) 2010 WL 5422405, at *10. Here, the essence of plaintiff's claim is that Mr. Srinivasan used the ROFR process in bad faith to prevent the condition precedent of the contract from occurring. Such conduct is proscribed by the language of section 3.1 requiring parties to use reasonable best efforts "to cause such conditions to be satisfied at the earliest reasonably possible date." Just like in Narrowstep, Inc. v. Onstream Media Corp, defendant's repeated bad faith manufacturing of delays in the closing of the merger was expressly proscribed by the provision requiring defendant's "reasonable best efforts to effect an expeditious closing of the merger." Id at 11. Furthermore, section 3.1 requires the reasonable best efforts of the parties "to satisfy their respective obligations with respect to the conditions set forth [in the contract]" and the non-exercise of the right to first refusal within a prescribed time is a condition listed in section 4.1. Plaintiff's allegations that Mr. Srinivasan breached the implied covenant by not complying provisions of the ROFR agreements is covered by and is duplicative of plaintiffs' breach of contract cause of action. Because plaintiffs have not shown how amending the pleading would cure its deficiencies, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)
